title: Maintaining passive stack ventilation after draughtproofing
subtitle: Keeping PSV grilles clear and effective as the house becomes more airtight
excerpt: Passive stack ventilation relies on warm moist air rising up a duct and escaping at roof level, drawing fresh air through grilles at low level. Draughtproofing and loft insulation work often inadvertently blocks these grilles or disrupts the temperature differential the system depends on. This explains how to check the system is still working and what to do when it is not.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Approved Document F (Ventilation, 2022 edition) for background ventilation requirements in existing dwellings. BRE Report BR 378 on passive stack ventilation design and performance. Energy Saving Trust ventilation guidance for airtightness improvements.

content.text: Passive stack ventilation (PSV) is found in many UK homes built or substantially refurbished from the 1970s onward: a vertical duct from a ceiling grille in a kitchen or bathroom rises through the roof space and exits via a ridge or slate ventilator at the roof covering. No fan; the system works because warm, moist air is less dense than cool, dry air, and rises naturally through a vertical column. On a cold night the stack works well. On a warm summer day with low wind, it barely functions.

Draughtproofing reduces the fresh-air supply that the stack draws against. Loft insulation can inadvertently bury the top of the duct or the roof-level exit terminal. Either problem reduces the flow rate through the stack, leading to higher moisture levels in bathrooms and kitchens, condensation on cold surfaces, and in severe cases mould growth within six months of the improvement work.
How to check the system is working

Locate the ceiling grilles. PSV grilles are typically 150 mm or 200 mm diameter, often in the ceiling of a bathroom, kitchen, or utility room. They may have a manually adjustable flap or a fully open fixed face.

Hold a piece of tissue paper against the grille on a cold day with the room at normal temperature. In a functioning system, the tissue should be pulled gently toward the grille and held lightly by suction. If it falls away, the stack is not drawing.

Check the roof-level exit. In the loft, trace the duct upward to its exit point. Confirm the exit terminal (a mushroom-headed cap or louvred box) is clear of insulation and positioned where roof air can circulate around it.

Check for obstructions in the duct run. PSV duct runs should be vertical or close to vertical. Horizontal sections, kinks, or duct that has been partially compressed by loft insulation boards all reduce flow.
Grille blocked with dust or grease: clean the grille by removing it (usually one or two screws) and washing it. Clean the first 200 mm of duct visible from the grille with a long brush.

Exit terminal buried under loft insulation: clear insulation from around the exit duct for a 300 mm radius. If needed, fit a duct extension to raise the terminal above the new insulation depth and refit the terminal on top.

Insufficient fresh-air supply after draughtproofing: the stack needs a source of replacement air. If all adventitious gaps have been sealed, open the trickle vent on the nearest external window by 3 mm. Alternatively, fit a discreet 50 mm background ventilator through an external wall at low level in the same room.

Stack does not work in summer or warm weather: this is normal. PSV depends on temperature differential. In warm weather, add a humidity-controlled mechanical extractor fan wired through the existing PSV grille opening. This supplements the passive system without adding new penetrations.

Approved Document F requires 5000 mm² of background ventilation per habitable room when a house undergoes a significant airtightness improvement. Each trickle vent or background ventilator provides roughly 2000-4000 mm² of equivalent area. A three-room house that has been draughtproofed needs at least three trickle vents open at all times.

Done well: the PSV grilles are clean, the exit terminal is clear of insulation, and the tissue-paper test shows suction on a cold day. Condensation on bathroom mirrors clears within 30 minutes of a shower. CO2 levels (measured with a monitor) stay below 800 ppm during normal occupancy.
Done badly: insulation buried over the duct exit, grilles painted shut, and all trickle vents closed. Condensation appears on cold corners within months of draughtproofing.

attrs.symptom: Bathroom shows mould in corners after draughtproofing work
attrs.cause: Reduced fresh air supply means shower moisture is not removed quickly enough
attrs.fix: Open the window trickle vent; clear the PSV grille. If this does not resolve within four weeks, fit a humidity-controlled extractor fan in addition to the PSV.

attrs.symptom: PSV duct terminates in the loft space rather than through the roof
attrs.cause: Duct was cut short during a previous refurbishment or was never extended to the roof level
attrs.fix: Extend the duct with a push-fit flexible connection and a new external terminal. The duct must exit through the roof covering; terminating in the loft is ineffective and deposits moisture into the loft space.
